nativeRange.habitat: Inshore and offshore coral reefs at depths of roughly 5-40 m, generally associated with branching corals.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indian Ocean coral reef among branching corals such as Acropora and Pocillopora, where small aggregations hover above the colonies to feed on zooplankton.

diet.notes: In the wild this is a planktivore that hovers above branching corals picking zooplankton from the water column. In captivity it generally accepts a varied mix of marine flakes and pellets, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafoods, and occasional herbivore preparations such as spirulina or dried marine algae.

avoidWith: Chromis and cardinalfish in small tanks
newly added tankmates after the damsel is established

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 4348x2899. Tack-sharp lateral portrait of a live, vibrant adult clearly showing the diagnostic dusky head, dark vertical body bars, iridescent blue-purple speckling, blue-edged dorsal, dark pelvic/anal fins and pale caudal peduncle/tail. Best overall specimen.

Storm Martin (via iNaturalist), 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ lateral of a single fish hovering over branching Acropora, matching the wild biotope; whole animal in frame with clear bars, blue spotting and white caudal peduncle.

Diego Delso, 6337x4225. High-res habitat shot over branching coral showing the shoaling context (a second background dascyllus); illustrates how it is kept/displayed. Fish slightly softer than the portrait, so kept as non-recommended.

summary: Small Indian Ocean damselfish, white-bodied with a dusky head and black-edged fins, that shoals over branching corals in the wild. Hardy and generally reef-safe, but tends to become territorial with age and is usually best kept singly.
careNotes: The Cloudy Damselfish is generally considered one of the easier marine fish for new saltwater keepers — broadly reef-safe with corals and invertebrates, and willing to accept prepared foods without much coaxing. Although a 30 US gal (~114 L) minimum is widely cited, a tank of around 120 L or larger is generally advised because adults tend to become territorial and benefit from open swimming space above the rockwork, supported by abundant live rock. Juveniles can sometimes be kept in small groups, or in a harem of one male with several females, in larger setups of roughly 200 L and up with plenty of broken sight-lines to reduce aggression; adults are more often kept singly because they tend to pick on conspecifics and smaller, timid tankmates. The species may occasionally nibble at very small bottom-dwelling crustaceans but does not harm corals. Wild reef populations live at temperatures of roughly 25.9–29°C, but the aquarium trade generally recommends a somewhat cooler range of about 22–26°C, which reflects keeper experience in closed systems.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair formation around spawning; demersal eggs are attached to a hard substrate and guarded and aerated by the male until hatching. Captive spawning has been reported but rearing the pelagic larvae through metamorphosis is generally considered difficult and is rarely attempted by hobbyists; captive-bred specimens are seldom seen in the trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection root for damselfish. Species' product page at /product/3207/ supplied care level (Easy), temperament (Aggressive), reef-compatible (Yes), water conditions (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025), 30 US gal minimum, max ~3 in, omnivore diet. Note: LiveAquaria mislabels the family as Chaetodontidae — corrected here to Pomacentridae per FishBase.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria/misc), max length 7.0 cm TL, depth 5-40 m, Indian Ocean distribution (East Africa, Mauritius, Réunion, Seychelles, Maldives, Sri Lanka, Andaman Sea, Java Sea), wild reef temperature 25.9-29°C, planktivore (trophic level 2.7), oviparous with male egg-guarding, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Quality Marine — Cloudy Dascyllus
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~3 in), tank (30 gal minimum), temperament (peaceful for genus as juveniles, more territorial with age), diet (meaty + spirulina), water parameters matching LiveAquaria (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, 8-12 dKH), reef-safe. Notes rarity of captive-bred specimens and tendency to nibble very small bottom crustaceans.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs form on spawning sites and the male guards demersal eggs on the substrate. Larval rearing through the pelagic phase is generally considered difficult and captive-bred specimens are rarely available in the trade.
